data.title: Adobe Looks Interesting At 7-Year Low (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My last Adobe Inc. (ADBE) article was a Sell suggestion in October 2023 here. For sure, avoiding the stock would have helped readers to sidestep a -48% price loss since then. Even worse news is the S&P 500 has gained +66% in price over the last 29 months. Investing elsewhere would have likely generated nice gains for investors. To visualize the difference, had you invested $1 in each, ADBE would be worth $0.52 today, while the plain-vanilla S&P 500 position would be worth $1.66. That's quite a spread.

The 2025-26 issue for Adobe has been growing fears of AI disruption in its highly profitable business model. The primary idea is that soon enough creative types will be able to utilize low- or no-cost AI instructions to create the same images and edits as those done with a subscription/purchase of Adobe software (alongside expertise in how to use such).

While the company will undoubtedly lose users in the future and may have to cut prices to keep others, it is developing and employing AI tools inside its applications as fast as it can. And, if management can stay ahead of the curve in terms of integrating technological advances, it may actually be a beneficiary of cutting-edge AI tools. Proactively adding AI efficiencies throughout its software ecosystem is the only way forward.

Anyway, the risk of AI buildout and its effects on Adobe are real. My question is how long it will take to meaningfully affect the bottom line. This may be a 5- or 10-year process for Adobe dealing with changes in how print/media editing is accomplished. It may be the company offers free or cheap subscriptions to many existing clients to keep them around (with outside advertisements drawn around your work screen, with revenue for Adobe), while still charging high-end fees to access the newest and coolest tools for print and media creation.

Will business income levels continue to expand at 15% to 25% annual clips? No, Wall Street has already downgraded expectations into the 5% to 15% range. A conservative projection may call for 0% to 10% numbers into 2030.

For an investment rating conclusion, the stock has become so inexpensive even flat to slight sales/income growth may lead to a minimum of equity market-typical returns for shareholders buying under $260. My thinking is investors are now buying a realistic expectation of minor growth rates, while upgraded upside will come for shareholders if Adobe can maintain EPS growth rates of 10% or above.

At this stage of the pricing cycle, Adobe has slid far enough to be considered a long-term Buy. I am upgrading my opinion to reflect this fact, using a 12-month outlook as my measuring period.

Let's review the valuation picture, which is the highlight excuse to research Adobe today. With sales and earnings rising robustly for decades, you would expect a sound valuation play to exist with the stock quote near a 7-year low (2019).

Lowest Valuation Since Great Recession

Honestly, I am pegging Adobe with its lowest valuation on "trailing" results and those expected for early 2026 since 2009, in the heat of the Great Financial Crisis and Recession. If you will, the stock is trading at a 17-year low valuation in the middle of February.

Reviewing the simple financial ratios of price to earnings, sales, and cash flow, ADBE may be a realistic bargain today, assuming AI disruption is handled smartly. You have to go back to 2012 to find a lower regime of 15.8x earnings, 4.7x sales, and 11.2x operating cash flow.

The free cash flow yield is incredibly attractive if earnings and cash flow remain unchanged in the years ahead. The trailing 8.6% FCF yield has only been bested by a wide margin for a few months in late 2008 and early 2009. Plus, the relative FCF rate vs. the "risk-free" 1-year Treasury equivalent (of 3.4%) is +5.2% in favor of Adobe ownership. Only the middle to final months of the Great Recession and 2012 supported a stronger upfront adjusted yield.

Considering the crazy funding needs for AI hyperscalers, on top of similar AI-use pressures on application software names or cloud-centric business models (SaaS), Adobe stands out today as a unique FCF play. It is now the number one pick for value-focused investors in the mega-cap Big Tech peer/competitor group I have drawn below. This is a far different setup than a few years ago, when Adobe was in the middle of the pack. 8.6% for FCF yield is super-sized vs. the peer median average around 2.9%.

Further, when we include changing debt and cash totals at Adobe, the EV to core cash EBITDA ratio is very appealing. You have to go back to the first-half of 2009 to find a similar 8.7x projection number or better vs. the early 2026 forecast.

Again, ADBE's enterprise valuation on EBITDA is dramatically more desirable than the alternatives you could pick for your investment dollar. 8.7x forward estimates is quite cheap vs. a peer median average of 15x.

Is the Ultra-Low Valuation Deserved?

This is the $64,000 question each reader must answer for themselves. Had AI disruption not existed, my answer would be a resounding NO. Why? Profit margins on sales are some of the highest in corporate America, and the growth outlook remains above average vs. the S&P 500.

Below is a graph of gross, operating, and final after-tax profit margins back to the late 1990s. Today's 88.6% gross, 36.6% operating, and 30% final profit margins remain above Adobe's long-term averages. In addition, these margins are in the middle to high-end of the peer sort group.

What about analyst estimates for growth? On this metric, expected earnings growth is on the weaker side of the peer group, but not amazingly so. 11%+ EPS gains compounded annually into 2028 are stronger than the long-term S&P 500 company average income growth rate under 10%.

Final Thoughts

One datapoint that does not fit a bullish narrative, which I absolutely don't like, is that insiders have been regular sellers of the stock at prices above $300, especially in January. The silver lining is volume totals have been relatively minor, but buy trades have been lacking. I would prefer some insiders step up to the plate if a true value proposition is presenting itself.

On the technical trading front, there is really nothing to put my finger on yet when searching for a price bottom or turnaround evidence. So, this is purely a Buy suggestion based on valuations and a contrarian bet that AI-related dumping is getting overdone. I will say, if the momentum picture improves, I will move my Adobe portfolio position from a small starter stake to something bigger for a weighting.

Don't let the multi-year low stock quote scare you either. I have picked many winners in the past at 5-year or even 10-year lows. Swings from extreme overvaluations to the opposite for an undervaluation setting are usually the cause of multi-year price lows at blue-chip large-cap businesses. If you will, pent-up value and a rebound in either expectations or operating growth can generate a very sizable rebound for investors willing to buy when others are dumping and running scared. Doubling, tripling, or even quadrupling your money may be your Adobe reward over the next 5-10 years if AI fears prove overblown.

What are the investment risks? The one we have been talking about is the AI revolution. The clear and present danger is that an AI application will soon be invented to generate editing features at little or no cost for consumers and businesses. It sounds a little fantastical that massive computing power matched with intelligent AI code can accomplish the same output as software technology developed over 30+ years. But anything is possible.

The second important downside risk to consider is the Big Tech space on Wall Street may eventually experience an all-out bust after decades of bubble building. This scenario may have better odds of becoming reality in my mind and could push ADBE's stock quote closer to $200 before a lasting bottom has been outlined (which would approximate a 30-year low valuation overall on trailing results).

Lastly, a deep recession scenario could push Adobe sales and earnings unexpectedly lower because businesses and consumers will cut back on spending of all sorts, including software they may only use on occasion.

Weighing all the pros and cons of stock ownership, I am confident today's low valuation will support price to a degree that offsets AI-related subscriber and software purchase losses. That's my view anyway, and the logic for me switching from a Sell rating at share pricing above $500 several years ago to a Buy outlook at $264.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Domino's Missed The Value Rotation
data.description:
As a rather unsophisticated investor, my primary goal is not necessarily to achieve outsized returns and outsmart the markets in absolute terms, as science is pretty clear on the fact that outperformance is unlikely A) consistently B) without insider knowledge, or C) without luck. As such, my portfolio aims at achieving an above-average Sharpe Ratio by capturing market-average returns over the long run while carrying significant below-average volatility. I achieve this with a stock-only approach by combining “boring” old-economy with high-growth tech titles, and buying both somewhat countercyclically, i.e. into relative weaknesses. Notably, stock selection comes on top of that, as we will see in a second.

But what has this got to do with Domino’s Pizza? Over the last month or so, tech has come under pressure, which led to a rotation of capital into “bubble-proof” consumer staples, lots of which I was bullish on in the recent year(s), and "good old" restaurant chains seem to have worked out as havens as well at first glance, don’t they? McDonald’s is up 8% in a month while the Nasdaq-100 is down 5%, equating to a 13% 1-month outperformance. Nevertheless, a look under the hood reveals that stock selection won in this case. Domino’s Pizza, for example, was down 8% during the same time, seeing an even more pronounced drop as the “tech-index.”

So, why is it that Domino’s Pizza could not at all profit from the market rotation? During my last DPZ coverage, I highlighted the company’s slightly elevated valuation relative to peers as well as its asset-light and debt-heavy balance sheet [BS]. While the former has now become less demanding thanks to the continued strong income and cash flow statements [IS & CFS] and the fallen share price, DPZ’s asset-light approach does not allow for huge earnings swings from operating leverage – low risk, low reward on that end. Compare that to McDonald’s real estate portfolio with likely significant off-balance sheet value.

I maintain a positively inclined Hold rating on DPZ, simply because I do not see why to choose a simple pizza franchise over other more comprehensive models in the sector. After all, DPZ continues not to rank among the reported top three chain restaurants for US teens, according to Piper Sandler. Chick-fil-A, McDonald’s and Chipotle hold the top spots, although even the number three does not appear to benefit from this mindshare. DPZ’s IS & CFS performance and attractive valuation, on the other hand, remains more than solid.

Quality Metrics and Leverage Review

With DPZ set to report earnings next week on 23 February pre-market, let us recap where the company and its stock stand today. These are some core characteristics of DPZ: the company boasts almost consistently high net income to free cash flow conversion of around 100%. Total shareholder payouts (dividends + buybacks) over the last 3 years have also stabilized around 100%, versus excessive debt-funded buybacks prior to that. TTM free cash flow margin has reached 13% as of Q3-25, which represents peak FCF margin performance, last seen during 2022.

Thanks to continued earnings growth and the more disciplined approach to leverage in recent years, net debt [ND] to EBITDA continued to decrease towards 5x, the lowest reading in almost a decade and interest coverage increased further to a ten-year high of 4.8x. This is a welcome trajectory for investors opposed to the high financial leverage, albeit ND to EBITDA is still stretched in absolute terms.

Both metrics were calculated by the author based on Seeking Alpha standard classifications for ND, EBITDA, and EBIT. Exact values might diverge from DPZ reported figures due to different standards of line-item classifications.

The overall peer group picture regarding debt has changed little since summer 2025, as is visible below. My peer group consists of McDonald’s, Chipotle Mexican Grill, Yum! Brands, Restaurant Brands International, Darden Restaurants, Domino’s Pizza, and Texas Roadhouse. DPZ continues to mark the outlier in terms of debt in % of assets, as the company’s interest-bearing debt exceeds its asset side of the balance sheet by three times. On the other hand, its strong free cash flow keeps Domino’s ranking below peer group median in terms of theoretical payback years.

Improved Valuation

Due to Domino’s notable recent underperformance relative to some sector peers, forward valuation multiples have shifted in favor of the company. While DPZ ranked as the somewhat more expensive peer back in summer 2025, it now represents one of the cheaper options with various 2028 FWD multiples being one to four points lower than the median peer. Please refer to the chart below for details.

Again, backward-engineering a DCF model based on consensus earnings estimates for the next couple of years, the historical FCF conversion of roughly 100%, and a CAPM-based WACC of 7.8%, the residual terminal FCF growth rate after 2027, which is baked into the company’s current valuation of $13 billion (down from $15 billion), is now less than 4% (down from 4.5%). Finally, let us have a look at the company’s current growth trajectory and whether it could meet this required residual growth rate going forward.

Growth Drivers

Domino’s reported strong growth of around 7% in global retail sales during Q3-25. Recent drivers have been an acceleration in domestic same store sales [SSS] growth, while international SSS slowed. On the other hand, DPZ’s international presence continues to be fueled by store expansion, with 185 net new international stores added during the quarter. As such, Domino’s international stores continue to drive the company’s growth and with that, SSS growth overseas might be even more important for the company than domestic SSS acceleration. Heading into Q4-25 earnings, I will therefore be eyeing international SSS growth in particular.

Takeaway

Domino’s recent financial IS and CFS performance remained solid and growth was recently driven by US SSS acceleration and international store expansion. For Q4-25 earnings, which are set to be reported next week, I will be looking at international SSS growth in order to see whether or not international penetration already tests the limits of per-store performance. The stock’s valuation setup is now far more intriguing than during summer 2025, although DPZ would still not be my top pick in the sector. The company’s financial leverage, lack of operating leverage, and personally perceived lack of brand moat keep me on the sidelines, while I do positively acknowledge the franchisor’s consistent free cash flow generation.

data.title: I'm An Uber Bull, For Now I Believe DoorDash Is Better
data.description:
I’m an Uber Bull, For Now I Believe DoorDash Is Better

I had a bit of an epiphany this week, watching Uber Technologies (UBER) break under 70 again. I was disappointed that the stock could not hold the upper 70s at least. Don’t get me wrong, I understand that UBER is under the same pressure as every other tech and tech-adjacent category of stocks, either that might be overtaken by Gen AI directly, or reduced subscription fees, or some new start-up challenger will come along and build a super cheap replacement using “Vibe-Coding.” I am sticking with UBER because it is the premier platform for ride-hailing. People will still need the super convenient App and Platform for travel. Uber is investing in self-driving tech right now, and many, if not all, of the Robotaxi fleets that pop up will have an easier time getting fares using the Uber App as their storefront. I am staying in UBER for the long haul.

As I was saying this week, while my discomfort over UBER was still fresh, I was scanning through my stock lists when my eye rested upon the DoorDash (DASH) symbol. I have been an admirer of the founder of DASH since I watched a video interview of him about a year ago. DASH was now trading at 175! I distinctly remembered DASH being over 250, which wouldn’t deter me, but I don’t like buying a stock at the 52-WH. At this time, while thinking it over, DASH fell to about 170 and had broken that support level. Causing me to hesitate a bit more. The stock landed on 160, which matches the level it fell to reach the April 2025 – “Liberation Day” chaos. I started a position at 160, realising that I made this decision purely on price action and charting, I realized that I had some work to do.

Serendipity started me on the road of comparing UBER to DASH.

Both seem to be competing strongly with each other. This is something that might be quite obvious, but I have considered the UBER app for ride-hailing, and DASH is for food delivery. With that built-in bias, this comparison forced me to dig deeper. Both DASH and UBER compete mightily in food delivery. Though it is a fact that most of Uber's revenue comes from ride-hailing. A full 57.1% of UBER's revenue is derived from Mobility(cite). Mobility is how UBER identifies the rideshare business. This does confer an advantage to UBER in support of its Uber Eats business. With revenue coming in from ride-hailing, funding the Uber Eats expansion. Though as we progress, we'll see that DASH acquits itself quite well by comparison. Let's push on and see.

UBER is now just 15 times P/E, DASH is 81.80 times!

So I start looking at the fundies, and I naturally start comparing UBER with DASH. DASH's current global expansion was my first thought. Also, P/E is a terrible indicator for stock selection or timing of a stock’s value. For instance, look at cyclical stocks. In some resource companies, like miners, the best time to buy the stock is when the P?E is very high. This, in my opinion, usually signals a bottom in that resource's value. The “P” stayed elevated, and the “E” dropped precipitously. So the time to put money into a resource stock is when it has a high P/E, not a relatively low one (to the market average). I just checked several copper mining stocks, and they all have P/Es in the high 30’s, which should mean that they aren’t done going up, or my thesis is incorrect. Here is an item in Investopedia that buttresses my counterintuitive claim. Just scroll down to the paragraph heading that says “Strategies for Investing in Cyclical Stocks” and read from there.

Once again, I stray off target, but it is important to put P/E in perspective. Some stocks deserve a higher P/E because they are growing faster. Stockmarket participants value revenue growth above all else. Witness the valuation of Anthropic now received funding at the $380B valuation. They have the prior 52 weeks' revenue run-rate at $13B that is 30X revenue, not earnings. There aren’t any earnings; in fact, they are burning cash, hence the multiple rounds of investment raises. The key performance here is revenue growth; both OpenAI and Anthropic are growing revenue at what might be historic rates. So the valuations are bid up and up. OpenAI's revenue run-rate for 2025 was $20B at 233% revenue growth over 2024 (cite). It is said that OpenAI is looking to go IPO at close to $1T valuation. Anthropic has a similar growth rate, albeit with a lower amount of revenue (cite).

Charting and Price.

The typical value investor doesn’t necessarily worry about the current price because they have already determined that the stock in question is underpriced. I think adhering just to the fundamentals leads to a lot of angst and second-guessing if the price action and the charts are ignored.

This is my methodology - Narrative, revenue and cash flow, then price, and the charts not in that order all the time. Narrative is extremely powerful, right now the narrative is that Gen AI is about to destroy every service and technology company in every sector. Perhaps I exaggerate, but I am skeptical that AI in all its forms is equivalent to the “3 Horses of the Apocalypse in devastating all instances of software technology. If any tech company is blind sided by a new competitor, it wasn’t only about the “Vibe Coding.” The majority of the established names have already embraced Vibe Coding, and the ones that haven’t will find themselves playing catch-up. I bought my first tranche of DASH at 160, and plan a number of tranches if and when the stock falls further. Did I put the cart before the horse, yes I did. I did shoot first. On the face of it, this appears to be a grave error. However, I am starting with a small position as I always do, and since the stock’s 52-week low is 155. That 52-Week low coincided with “Liberation Day” chaos, and certainly DASH is a better company now than it was then.

So I took a shot, perhaps the timorousness over the narrative of AI eating all of technology is imagined as a greater possible calamity to the economy than the aforementioned “Liberation Day.” Let’s just say they are equal, that still means we are close to or at a bottom for DASH. So let me refine the basis for this comparison; all I need to do is show that DASH is putting up stronger revenue growth. The truth is I would buy DASH even if UBER is at the same growth rate, since I am not selling any shares of UBER at the moment and would continue to buy it if it falls further.

Okay, let’s chart DASH. I had to go out 5-years on the chart to show decent support, after it broke the first dotted line. As it stands right now, DASH is resting at the 160 line (second dotted line), the 3rd thin line is a bit more support at 155. This value level goes back several years. Surely you would agree that today DASH is a better stock than it was nearly 5 years ago? So understand price and the charts can give one some basis for taking a position.

Now let’s take a good look at the financial performance. I don’t recall any bad news that would truly put the kybosh on the stock, except some snag with the NY City authorities about tipping (cite). I would expect this development to make more of an impression on DASH’s value, in my opinion, than UBER, since UBER’s larger global footprint makes it less affected by any one city’s regulation. Let me stress that this is not an either-or proposition; I am investing in both names. My overriding supposition is that the death of enterprise software, or any software-driven service like UBER or DASH, is a huge task. Both of these App-Driven businesses are two-sided; you have to grow drivers, and you have to grow the network of consumers by being the most convenient tool to satisfy the huge demands of modern society, getting from A to B, and eating from A through Z in cuisine, on an App and receiving nourishment or transport superconveniently. This is very hard to do; to grow two such networks simultaneously.

DASH navigates the AI threat by coopting it. On December 15, 2025, DASH announced a partnership with OpenAI to launch a DoorDash app inside ChatGPT (cite) that lets users turn recipe ideas directly into grocery orders fulfilled via the company's local delivery network. This opens another opportunity for DASH’s move into delivering items other than restaurant meals. I like this nuanced partnership because it allows ChatGPT to do what it does best: understand text and sift out the important pieces automatically, setting up the order for delivery.

DASH Third Quarter 2025 Key Financial Metrics (cite)

  • Total Orders increased 21% Y/Y to 776 million.

  • Marketplace GOV increased 25% Y/Y to $25.0 billion.

  • Revenue increased 27% Y/Y to $3.4 billion.

  • GAAP net income attributable to DoorDash, Inc. common stockholders increased 51% Y/Y to $244 million.

  • Adjusted EBITDA increased 41% Y/Y to $754 million.

Note: DASH reports Q4 on February 18, this Wednesday.

Here is the relevant text;

“In Q3 2025, DoorDash drove accelerated year-over-year (Y/Y) growth in revenue, Total Orders, and Marketplace GOV. Our ability to generate growth in our business through strong execution and consistent reinvestment drives our ability to positively impact local economies. In Q3 2025, we generated nearly $24 billion in combined sales for merchants and earnings for Dashers, and we expect to generate well over $100 billion in 2026. We believe we are still in our early stages and are energized by the opportunity to continue learning, building, and investing on behalf of consumers, merchants, Dashers, and our shareholders.”

“In our U.S. new vertical categories, we expanded selection in Q3 2025, including key new partnerships in the grocery and retail categories. We also recently launched DashMart Fulfillment Services, which provides infrastructure that we believe will enable our partners and us to provide more consistent and higher-quality consumer experiences.” So DASH is expanding not only geographically but also into other adjacent categories, like groceries and personal care categories as well (cite). In my opinion, DASH is coming after Maplebear (CART) territory, but also being a close follower to UBER attacking similar adjacencies.

We are comparing DASH Q3 to UBER Q4

This is not a totally fair comparison. On Wednesday, we will know for sure, but for now, let's assume a similar result for DASH's Q4 as it did for Q3. Here is an interesting article on Yahoo Finance, which says that DASH is expected to deliver in-line results. Also, it is quite interesting that the article compares DASH with UBER as well. The article further noted that UBER did well in Q4 in delivery, so too should DASH. Here's a quote: "Data from Bank of America’s debit and credit card tracking showed US online restaurant spending was up 8% year-over-year in Q4, stable versus Q3, while Uber’s recent results suggest retail is accelerating delivery growth." Another interesting and relevant quote.

"BofA sees the recent sector selloff as overdone. “We think Q1 margin concerns have been building since Q3 results, and Q1 outlook could be a clearing event if top line is strong & full year margin outlook is relatively unchanged,” they wrote. They also highlighted limited AI risk for DoorDash given its strong courier, restaurant, and subscription network." (cite)

Global expansion for DASH is how it is setting up to compete strongly with UBER

DASH is indeed in the early stage of globalizing its service, having recently acquired Deliveroo, a London-based delivery business, for $3.9B in cash. (cite) This is one of several acquisitions in global markets for food delivery, matching UberEats moves. Deliveroo serves nine countries, including France, Italy, Qatar, Singapore, and the United Arab Emirates. This follows another acquisition in 2022, DASH bought Wolt, a Finnish food delivery company that operated in 23 countries, primarily in Europe, for about $8.1 billion in 2022. (cite)

It appears to me that two world-class operators, which UBER and DASH plainly are, are competing with each other vigorously. Something I didn’t recognize until now.

Looking at UBER for comparison

I am surprised to learn that UBER is filing for a mixed shelf offering. No other details were revealed. This was announced after the Friday close, so it’s the first I’m learning of this. Interesting that the UBER CEO earlier announced additional buybacks, only to dilute the shares now? Also interesting that this Friday UBER broke under 70. Am I intimating that some market participants heard this news early? Heaven forbid! I am merely pointing out this coincidence. The 52-week low goes back to the April “Liberation Day” at 60.63, and as I said, broke under 70 for the first time since then. Here is the chart.

This is far from a beautiful chart. UBER has broken through a lot of support lines. On Friday, the price stopped at minor support at 69-ish (thin red dotted line). There is better support at the next black dotted line at about 65. We know that, that line will also be tested to take into account the surprising news that UBER is doing a secondary offering that will likely dilute the shares.

To my eye, the DASH chart looks better, though both charts are not great. In defense of UBER it has been subject to the Gen AI narrative more tightly (in my opinion), attached to the AI controversy than DASH. Though some might say there will be robo-delivery at some point to challenge the Dasher model.

It’s just too soon to put it into any kind of valuation for now. There needs to be a huge amount of development and social engineering to enable an unmanned conveyance with free food to be had for the taking. Also, going up stairs, and other physical obstacles, for these reasons, this is not happening for at least another 10 to 20 years. I can say that, in my opinion, a lot of the retreat in price is just the overall selling pressure of these kinds of stocks.

Uber is addressing the robotaxi issue

On February 13, UBER, along with WeRide Inc. (WRD), announced the launch of Abu Dhabi’s first commercial robotaxi service in the city center/ Starting today, Uber riders traveling to or from the newly added areas may be matched with a WeRide GXR Robotaxi via UberX or Uber Comfort, or book directly through the app’s dedicated “Autonomous” option. Waymo has partnered with UBER on several new cities outside of San Francisco. July 2925 UBER signs a $300M deal with Lucid (LCID) for self-driving vehicles for the next 6-years. (cite)

UBER Q4 reported mixed fourth-quarter 2025 results, with the earnings missing the Consensus Estimate while revenues surpassed the mark. Quarterly earnings per share of 71 cents missed the Consensus Estimate of 79 cents and declined 77.8% year over year.

Total revenues of $14.3 billion were higher than the consensus estimate of $14.2 billion. The top line jumped 20.1% year over year on a reported basis and 19% on a constant currency basis (cite). I won’t ding UBER on the earnings miss; they are spending on tuck-in acquisitions, and research and development creating new services, to battle formidable rivals. The best way to assess earnings is by the adjusted EBITDA, which in the fourth quarter surged 35% year over year to $2.48 billion. So the earnings picture is really much better than the simple earnings miss. Still, DASH has a cleaner earnings report in my opinion, especially when paired with the surprise secondary. It remains to be seen on their Q4 earnings report whether the earnings and revenue performance still put DASH in the lead.

UBER reported an impressive Q4 earnings report, but DASH is the Winner.

On February 4th, UBER reported a 20% revenue jump, a great result, but not close to DASH’s Q3 revenue report of 27% (see above). Also, this unexpected announcement of a secondary “Mixed Shelf” offering will likely result in further weakness in UBER. While DASH has its challenges, it currently has the edge in my mind. However, they are both pretty close in strategy and tactics. I only just started my position in DASH, and now even more excited to add to the position. The main thing that UBER has going for it over DASH is the ride share business, which provides 57.1% of UBER's revenue, a lot of cash to fund more services that compete with DASH. Does DASH make an acquisition in the ride-share space to even the odds? Or perhaps DASH leadership believes that sticking with just delivery solidifies the brand and sharpens their ability to execute.

In any case, I am going to hold off adding to UBER for now and wait to see what happens with this secondary offering (cite). I am trying to be even-keeled about it, but I am miffed to learn this after the Friday close. This is an old ploy; to release the news at Friday’s close and hope that somehow no one will notice, or maybe shareholders would cool off on this 3-day weekend. It is not working for me.

Other investments this week:

I added to my Amazon (AMZN) position once it fell to my 20% discount requirement. If it falls more I will add more. Last April I was buying the big-cap tech names, and I was well rewarded. Whatever this turbulence is about, I believe that AMZN will be one of the large-cap tech names leading the way. Andy Jassy has already proven himself to be a good steward of AMZN, and I know he can navigate through these choppy waters.I invested in more ServiceNow (NOW) equity. I believe that if any incumbent platform is going to shine in the development of the “Agentic Age”, it will be NOW. The stock has a super earnings report and is selling above the “Rule of 40”. CEO Bob McDermott was quoted as saying NOW is currently selling at the “Rule of 55+.” (cite) McDermott is at the top of his game, and I am adding to my position. The only answer to the bears in all this is financial performance, especially new revenue growth. If NOW continues to perform this well, the shares will be rewarded.

The names with built-in moats like NOW, AMZN, DASH, or UBER all have proprietary data and complex multi-party constituents that will be very difficult to replicate, and they execute at a very high level of competence. In my opinion, they have deep domain knowledge and strong corporate cultures that promote excellence.

The only way Gen AI-assisted rivals will take away their primacy is if the corporate culture gets diluted and execution suffers, but that danger is with all incumbents. Finally, I believe that all these names are closing in on their ultimate support levels, so they are a good risk.

Good luck, readers. We will weather this storm of fear over Gen AI, as illogical as it is. Spring is almost on its way!

data.title: Runway Growth Finance's BDC Bond, 7.1% Yield
data.description:
Thesis

Runway Growth Finance Corp. (RWAY) is a business development company with a $315 M market capitalization. Late last year the company announced the acquisition of SWK Holdings Corporation (SWKH):

MENLO PARK, Calif., Oct. 09, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Runway Growth Finance Corp. (Nasdaq: RWAY) ("Runway Growth" or the "Company"), a leading provider of flexible capital solutions to late- and growth-stage companies seeking an alternative to raising equity, today announced that it has entered into a definitive merger agreement to acquire SWK Holdings Corporation (Nasdaq: SWKH) ("SWK"), a life science focused specialty finance company that provides minimally dilutive financing to small- and mid-sized commercial-stage healthcare companies.

The acquisition has not closed yet, scheduled for March 2026 as per the latest projections.

In today's article we are going to cover the latest balance sheet developments for RWAY, as well as the origination of a new baby bond for the company.

Debt re-financing

Prior to the most recent balance sheet re-shuffling the company had the following debt structure:

The 7.5% 2027 Notes trade under the ticker RWAYL, while the 8% 2027 Notes trade under the ticker RWAYZ. Please also note the April 2026 maturity for roughly $25 M in bonds.

The BDC placed $100 M of new notes which have generated proceeds necessary to redeem the April maturity as well as refinance expensive debt:

MENLO PARK, Calif., Jan. 27, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Runway Growth Finance Corp. today announced that it has priced an underwritten public offering of $100.0 million aggregate principal amount of notes due 2031 (the "Notes"), which will result in net proceeds to the Company of approximately $97.0 million after payment of underwriting discounts and commissions. The Notes will mature on February 3, 2031 and may be redeemed in whole or in part at any time or from time to time at the Company's option on or after February 3, 2028. The Notes will be issued in denominations of $25 and integral multiples of $25 in excess thereof and will bear interest at a rate of 7.25% per year, payable quarterly, with the first interest payment occurring on March 1, 2026.

Before we get into the details of the new debt, please note the impact to the existing exchange traded debt:

  • RWAYZ will be fully redeemed
  • RWAYL will be redeemed partially (roughly 50% of the notional)

Holders of those baby bonds would have already received notices via their brokerage companies regarding the redemptions.

New debt considerations

The BDC placed a large amount of new debt because it could obtain a lower cost of funds. RWAYZ was paying 8%, while the new notes have a 7.25% coupon. Let us look at the characteristics for the new debt:

  • Notional: $100 M
  • Ticker: RWAYI
  • Coupon: 7.25%
  • Payment period: quarterly
  • Call date: Feb 3, 2028
  • Maturity date: Feb 3, 2031
  • Current price: $25.15
  • Yield to maturity: 7.1%

The new bonds come with a 2-year non-call period, and a yield to maturity of 7.1%. The yield is lower than the coupon because the new bonds have traded above par already.

The company has demonstrated fiscal responsibility and stability when it comes to managing its balance sheet, and we expect new issuance later in the year to take out the remaining RWAYL as well as the debt from the acquired company. If all goes as planned (i.e. the Fed cuts as expected), we should see even lower coupons on the expected new debt.

Company positioning and financial metrics

RWAY is set to diversify its portfolio via the latest acquisition:

RWAY is set to acquire a portfolio of loans which is very much focused towards the pharma & life sciences sector, a segment which is not currently present in the RWAY portfolio. The acquisition is set to add granularity and diversification for RWAY, as well as make the company larger.

RWAY has done a fair job in the past five years when it comes to share price performance and total return:

While the share price is lower, the total return is in the high teens. Also to note that January 2025, BC Partners Credit (the credit arm of the $40 billion global firm BC Partners) completed its acquisition of Runway Growth Capital LLC, which is the investment adviser that manages RWAY. This simply means there are more avenues to generate revenue, and there are vested interests in the long-term viability of the BDC.

The company has been delivering a solid net income in the past few years, with very little volatility:

The BDC has been solidly profitable, with a constant reach for higher profitability and assets in the past two years, hence the partnership with BC.

When it comes to debt holders, investors in this part of the capital structure should be mainly concerned with coverage ratios and relative valuation versus triggers. While common equity gets impacted by impairments first, a healthy coverage ratio for debenture holders ensures safety. RWAY has been reporting very good coverage ratios:

Pursuant to the 1940 Act, we are permitted to incur borrowings, issue debt securities, or issue preferred stock if, immediately after the borrowings or issuance, the ratio of total assets (less total liabilities other than indebtedness) to total indebtedness plus preferred stock is at least 150%. As of September 30, 2025 and December 31, 2024, our asset coverage ratio was 209% and 192%, respectively.

The trigger here is 150% for the BDC to be prevented from paying interest to the common shareholders. Any coverage ratio above 180% is on the healthy side, allowing for significant asset write-downs during a recession before the trigger is hit.

Conclusion

RWAYI is a new baby bond from Runway Growth Finance. The company has raised new debt to refinance expensive baby bonds and retire an April 2026 maturity. The company financials and coverage ratios are healthy, while the latest acquisition is supposed to close in March 2026. RWAYI comes with a 2-year non-call period and presents an attractive entry point currently at a 7.1% yield to maturity.

data.title: Adobe: A Broken Stock, But Not A Broken Company
data.description:
Adobe's (ADBE) stock has been selling off significantly over the past two years due to the 'AI fear trade'. The idea behind the AI fear trade is that artificial intelligence could replace traditional software applications. The fear also comes from the idea that AI disruption could lead to an increase in competition for similar applications. Another fear is that AI could disrupt the demand for software-as-a-service [SaaS] subscriptions. I will explain in the article how AI is more likely to benefit Adobe rather than negatively impact the company.

While I can't say for sure when the exact bottom will be in, I do think that Adobe's stock is bottoming as it reached a demand zone where the stock rallied from previously. The other issue for the stock has been the need for a re-valuation from an overvalued level. ADBE is now trading at attractive valuation levels.

How Adobe Can Thrive In The AI Age

Adobe is already integrating AI into its current applications. This can enhance the user experience while still providing highly professional results. By integrating AI into existing applications, Adobe can improve its offerings and make them more efficient. If Adobe is successful with its AI integration, it could help the company to maintain a high customer retention rate. It could also help drive the growth of new customers.

Frankly, it doesn't make sense for other AI-based entities to create alternatives to Adobe Acrobat, Creative Cloud, GenStudio, Photoshop, Presenter or any of the company's other software applications via AI. It does make sense for AI to be incorporated into these existing applications by Adobe to improve them and to remain competitive. So, we will probably see more partnerships between traditional software companies like Adobe with AI-based companies.

Adobe integration of AI is found in its Firefly offering. Adobe considers Firefly to be a commercially-safe application for generative AI output. Adobe partnered with other entities such as Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Open AI, Black Forest Labs, Runway, and others to create Firefly. The Firefly Image 5 model offers generation quality 4-megapixel resolution with industry-leading prompt-based editing features.

Adobe is measuring the usage and monetization of its AI through generative credits. The generative credits are a great indicator of high-value usage and credit consumption. Adobe measured a 3x yoy increase in Q4 2025 over Q4 2024 in high-value usage and credit consumption. As subscribers consume more generative credits, they can upgrade to higher-value offerings such as Creative Cloud or obtain Firefly credit add-ons.

I do have to acknowledge that Anthropic's agentic AI is one example of a new competitor for Adobe. Anthropic's Claude Cowork can process information to create summaries/reports. It can also create slide presentations from collections of data and it can organize and rename files & images. This competes with Adobe's Acrobat AI assistant, Adobe Express, Bridge, and other apps.

Canva is another competitor that uses AI tools to perform similar tasks as Adobe's offerings. Canva's Affinity software can handle photo editing, advanced graphic design, desktop publishing, image and video generation, etc. These all compete with Adobe's diverse array of products.

Another competitor, Figma (FIG), specializes in vector-based web and app prototyping/design which competes with Adobe XD. Figma's FigJam and Figma Slides compete with Adobe's Express & Miro for brainstorming, wireframing, and presentations/slide decks.

Adobe's products are used by many creative professionals. These professionals need polished end products that they can trust. It wouldn't make sense to switch to another creative AI tool over one that is already trusted. If the one that is trusted uses AI to create the desired polished professional documents, then those applications will probably continue to be trusted.

Adobe is already the expert for its creative applications. So, the company can probably integrate AI as well or better than any newcomers trying to outcompete them. ADBE has a good handle on what its users demand. So, they can continue to improve those products using AI to help increase user productivity. Incorporating AI can help provide a better overall value for its customers.

Valuation

Adobe's significant stock decline over the past few years brought the valuation back down to an attractive level. ADBE is trading at only 11.3x expected EPS of $23.46 for FY26. This is significantly below the sector median of 22.9. ADBE is also trading with a low PEG ratio of 0.87, which is below the sector median of 1.46.

I would typically expect a strong growth company like Adobe to be trading with a PEG at least above one. Many of the growth stocks that I cover tend to perform well when trading with PEG ratios between one and two. So, when I see one trading below one, it shows an attractively low undervaluation.

The expected EPS of $23.46 for FY26 would be 12% growth over FY25. This can be achieved with revenue growth of 9% to 10%. Then, EPS can get a boost from the margin expansion that Adobe has been achieving. The gross margin increased from 89.04% to 89.27% from FY24 to FY25. The operating margin increased from 36.36% to 36.63% and the net income margin increased from 25.85% to 30% over the same time.

The chart of Adobe's forward PE above shows the sharp decline in the valuation over the past two years. While the stock was arguably overvalued back in the beginning of 2024, it is now attractively undervalued at these levels. This should help bring buyers back in and put upward pressure on the stock.

Technical Perspective

Adobe's monthly chart above illustrates the drastic decline in the stock. The good news is that the last time the stock dropped below $300 back in 2022, it rallied back to over $600 within a year. So, the current price range could act as a demand zone for the stock. This is where many buyers could come in and drive the stock price back up.

The RSI is about at the same low level as where it was back in 2022 before the stock rallied. The MACD also dropped to a similar level to where it was before the 2023 rally.

It is just a matter of time when buyers realize the bargain level where the stock is currently trading. The AI fear trade is overdone in my opinion. Investors are likely to see the value for the stock. This is likely to spark the next rally. I can't pick an exact bottom, but the stock is getting to the point where the selling is becoming unsustainable.

Risks For Adobe

AI disruption is the biggest risk for the company over the long-term. It is possible that separate AI tools can be developed to perform the same tasks as Adobe's applications. Anthropic's agentic AI through Claude Cowork that I discussed earlier is one example of this. Adobe needs to continually innovate to remain a step ahead of any AI or other competition. There is no guarantee that Adobe's integration of AI will be more successful than competing AI tools.

Competition from traditional non-AI companies is another risk for Adobe. Affinity which is owned by Canva is the company's main competitor. Affinity offers similar applications as Adobe such as photo editing, desktop publishing, graphic design, etc. Adobe also competes with other companies such as Figma, Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet, and Salesforce (CRM). Increases in competition could take market share away from Adobe and lead to a lower future growth rate.

Adobe's Long-Term Outlook

I do acknowledge that AI could disrupt Adobe's business over the long-term. AI tools could be developed that perform the same functions as Adobe's applications. However, it will probably take multiple years for AI to reach a point where it can produce the quality, trusted, commercially-safe professional products that Adobe currently produces.

In the meantime, Adobe's integration of AI can help improve its current products and help maintain customer retention for the long-term. Adobe has the chance to effectively use AI to its advantage since they are already the experts in their application development of professional creative tools.

I expect the stock to rebound strongly in 2026 as it sits near an oversold condition and a major support zone on the monthly chart. The current low valuation is not likely to be ignored for much longer among investors. Adobe's strong expected double-digit earnings growth is likely to drive the stock for above-average gains in 2026 from this low valuation level.

data.title: VNQI: Easy Gains Made As International REITs Now Trade At A P/B Of 1x
data.description:
Introduction

The Vanguard Global ex-US Real Estate Index ETF (VNQI) is off to a strong start in 2026, benefiting from a decline in bond yields, as well as further weakness in the U.S. dollar (DXY). This has pushed the P/B ratio of VNQI to 1x, up from 0.9x when I last covered the ETF.

While I anticipate the pace of VNQI gains to slow down from the stellar returns achived in 2025 and 2026, I still see the ETF as an attractive risk-reward investment opportunity, confirming my previous Buy rating on the ETF. I estimate VNQI's potential return over the long term at about 7.78%, driven by:

  • A relatively safe 4.27% current dividend yield.
  • The potential to achieve roughly 3% dividend growth under baseline economic assumptions.
  • A circa 0.5-0.75% tailwind from incremental U.S. dollar weakness post 2026.

Investors not familiar with VNQI may want to check out the ETF Overview section in my previous article. Relative to September 2025, the ETF's allocation to the Pacific and Emerging markets is margnially lower, offset by an increase in "Other" regions.

Valuation update

After strong returns over the past year, international REITs held in VNQI now trade at a P/B of 1x. As a reminder, since international REITs generally prepare reports under IFRS (which requires regular revaluation of investment properties, usually every six months), P/B is a much more relevant metric to look at compared with U.S. REITs, which hold property at cost minus accumulated depreciation.

Assuming an average of 40% debt in international REIT capital structures (a common benchmark for REITs seeking an investment-grade credit rating) and 2% inflation, we now see that valuation gains should drive a circa 3% capital gain for VNQI holders, down from the 3.7% I estimated when VNQI holdings were trading at a P/B of around 0.9x. The silver lining is that VNQI holdings' creditors now have a larger equity cushion in terms of market capitalization, potentially allowing VNQI holdings to acquire cheaper financing.

Dividend remains attractive

Since a large portion of VNQI's recent gains were driven by a weak U.S. dollar, the ETF's dividend yield (dependent on dividends received in currencies such as the JPY, GBP, EUR, and AUD) has held up reasonably well, sitting at 4.27% in February 2026.

Dividend growth over the past decade stands at 3.96%, which I think could be largely replicated over the next decade when we factor in dividend growth of circa 3% thanks to rent indexation, as well as a small tailwind from a weaker U.S. dollar, which I will discuss below.

USD Outlook

With Fed funds futures markets pricing in a Fed funds rate of about 3% at the end of 2026, I think the bulk of the anticipated Fed easing this year is already reflected in a weaker U.S. dollar.

Even so, I expect the Fed funds rate to remain notably above interest rates in Japan, Singapore, Switzerland, Sweden, and the Eurozone post 2026, which account for roughly 45.5% of VNQI holdings. I believe this could realistically drive a 0.5-0.75% annual tailwind for VNQI dividends. My thinking is that currencies in these regions will rise in value to make up for higher USD interest rates, negating carry trade benefits over the long term.

VNQI Rating

To sum up, here are the key return drivers for VNQI as of February 2026:

Return component Percentage contribution Commentary
Dividend +4.27% Relatively safe as REITs typically strive to avoid dividend cuts
Capital appreciation/dividend growth +3% More uncertain, dependent on economic developments
Currency tailwind from a weaker U.S. dollar +0.5-0.75% Most uncertain, dependent on cross-border capital flows and monetary policy
Expense ratio -0.12% Acceptable for a long-term passive holding
Total ~7.78% Attractive for a defensive investment

Source: Author calculations

As you can tell from the table above, I still see VNQI delivering a total return of around 7.78% annually over the long term even after we factor in the 0.12% expense ratio.

With stock markets at all-time highs, I think this is an attractive return proposition for a defensive sector such as REITs. In essence, the incremental return one may get from adding more cyclical sectors to a portfolio might not be worth the risk.

To conclude, while the 7.78% return over the long term is likely to be well below gains achieved in 2025 and 2026, from a risk-reward perspective, I still see VNQI as a reasonable pick for investors principally looking for a solid dividend, perhaps complemented by the potential for incremental capital and currency gains. As such, I confirm my Buy rating on VNQI.

Risks

The principal risk in the VNQI investment case is a rise in long-term interest rates. This will not only drive higher funding costs (thus putting pressure on dividend growth), but also affect valuations through higher appraisal yields. Here the principal risk would be Japan as it is VNQI's largest holding at 23.1% of ETF assets. Readers may find the Debt and fiscal stimulus section in my recent WisdomTree Japan SmallCap Dividend Fund ETF (DFJ) article interesting in this respect.

The other principal risk to note is that dividend growth/capital gains remain dependent on economic developments in countries where VNQI invests. For instance, a severe recession is likely to have a negative impact on commercial property fundamentals, only partially offset by associated central bank easing to combat an economic slump. While real estate is generally perceived as a low risk investment, it is certainly not risk free. Hence readers looking for capital preservation at all cost should avoid VNQI.

Last but not least, while my baseline outlook is for a marginally weaker U.S. dollar post 2026, a rally in the dollar is likely to have a highly negative effect on U.S.-based VNQI investors. In essence, while VNQI is traded in USD, its underlying value depends on the prices of international REITs traded in currencies such as the JPY, AUD, and EUR.

Conclusion

The VNQI ETF is off to a strong start in 2026, driven by a decline in bond yields and further weakness in the U.S. dollar index. This has pushed VNQI holdings' valuations to a P/B of 1x, up from 0.9x when I last covered the ETF.

I estimate that VNQI's long term return potential stands at around 7.78% annually, notably below returns achieved in 2025 and 2026. Even so, I see this as an attractive risk-reward investment opportunity with stock markets at all-time highs, confirming my previous Buy rating on the ETF.

Key risks in the VNQI investment case include a rise in long-term interest rates, economic weakness putting pressure on commercial propery fundamentals, as well as a reversal of recent U.S. dollar losses.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: WEC Energy Group: Earnings Potential Tied To Capital Plan Execution
data.description:
American electricity and natural gas company WEC Energy Group, Inc. (WEC) recently reported its Q4 2025 and FY 2025 earnings, which showed that full-year earnings per share of $5.27 met the guidance issued earlier in 2025. The company is expected to execute its $37.5 billion capital plan between 2026 and 2030, which will also include the $1 billion capital injection intended to meet the 3.9 gigawatt power demand by both Vantage and Microsoft data centers. That said, I will explain why I am rating this stock as a hold, with the belief that the company has an upside potential of more than 40% tied to its capital plan, as outlined below.

In its 5-year capital plan execution, WEC projects a long-term earnings growth potential (or CAGR) of 7% to 8% (a midpoint of 7.5%) between 2026 and 2030. For FY 2026 and assuming normal weather conditions, WEC reaffirmed its earnings guidance in the range of $5.51 to $5.61- a midpoint of $5.56. To calculate the future earnings per share after the 5 years for WEC, I will add 1 to the CAGR, 7.5% or 0.075, and raise the total (1.075) to the power of 5 (the number of years), then multiply it by the initial earnings value of $5.56. My calculation shows that WEC’s earnings per share after the 5-year capital plan will be about $8, representing an increase of 43.9% (from 2026 to 2030).

In 2026, WEC projected that retail electric sales in Wisconsin would rise by 1.6% (YoY), with the data center loads forcing a 5.8% (YoY) power demand increase in the commercial and industrial segment. This growth forecast is in line with the EIA’s prediction of a 1% (YoY) electricity consumption growth in the US, followed by a 3% (YoY) increase in 2027, driven by surging power demand used by large computer centers. In FY 2025, electric consumption in residential areas grew 3.5% (YoY), with overall electric sales rising 1.4% (YoY) to 44,059.5 MWh. We are looking at a new load that is in line with the additional forecasted customer demand of 500 MW from Microsoft, intended to drive the forecast to 2.6 GW, and a further 1.3 GW from Vantage data centers, bringing the total demand to 3.9 GW by 2030. Last month, Vantage entered into a strategic partnership with Liberty Energy that would see the latter “deliver up to 1 GW of power by 2030 with 400 MW of power generation capacity reserved in 2027.” This will, in turn, help Vantage offer digital infrastructure through its data centers and propel hyperscale growth. It also comes after Vantage signed a $1.6 billion equity investment in an Asia-Pacific project, with part of the money used to close “a 300 MW+ hyperscale data center campus” deal in Malaysia.

It is worth noting that while Vantage will be "underwriting 100% of the power infrastructure" for the data center project, which essentially entails covering the project's cost, WEC will still handle the power distribution revenue. This will be done through its subsidiary, We Energies. In its announcement made in Q4 2025, Vantage stated,

"Vantage is underwriting 100% of the power infrastructure investment with a dedicated electricity rate from WEC Energy Group utility We Energies. This proposed rate is designed to protect other customers from any price increases from the new investments to serve Lighthouse."

WEC confirmed in its Q4 2025 and FY 2025 report that Vantage was planning to invest at least $15 billion for this project, with the first phase scheduled for completion in 2028. WEC will be handling the power distribution, bundling together the generation costs, and integrating them into the tariffs. Rather than point to a case of load defection, WEC is projecting that power demand at the Vantage site could rise from 1.3 GW to 3.5 GW (in the five years to 2030). Growth in power generation also means an increase in earnings over the years.

Back to WEC, of the $37.5 billion capital expenditure plan (from 2026 to 2030), about $20.3 billion will be allocated towards electric generation (representing 54.13% of the intended expenditure). This amount shows the addition of an extra $1 billion since the November 2025 CapEx plan, which indicated the company had set aside $19.3 billion for electric generation (giving the total of $36.5 billion as CapEx at the time). In turn, the sales forecast for the electric segment for 2028 to 2030 is expected to grow by 6% to 8%, higher than that of gas, set at 0.7% to 1.0% in the forecast period. I believe the lower forecast for gas is due to deflation, taking into account refining costs and profits. With full-year 2025 revenues at $9.8 billion, the upside in electric and gas sales is likely to raise revenue to at least $11 billion by 2028.

WEC’s annual dividend yield of 3.42% is also 3.88% higher than that of the sector median, which sits at 3.29%. While it has consistently raised its dividend for 23 consecutive years, its 4-year average dividend of 3.34% is about 9% lower than the sector average of 3.68%. I believe FY 2026 will be no less different, and we are likely to see further dividend increases since the company announced in January 2026 that it had grown the dividend rate by 6.7%, with the annualized rate now at $3.81. WEC has grown its dividend at 6.89% (TTM), which is 35.63% higher than the sector median of 5.08%, making this stock attractive to income investors. It is also vital to remember that WEC is targeting a dividend payout of 65% to 70%, as indicated in its FY 2025 earnings report, meaning that an increase in earnings, as elucidated herein, will continue to grow shareholder value for the company. The company set a target of 6.5% to 7% in 2026, which, in my view, is achievable.

Valuation

I will use the price-to-earnings growth (PEG) ratio to value WEC Energy since it will establish the stock’s value alongside its earnings growth, as emphasized in this article.

In my earlier calculations, I showed that WEC Energy is expected to grow its EPS to about $8 (the projected EPS) following the execution of its $37.5 billion 5-year capital plan. I also explained that the forward annual EPS growth rate over that period stood at a mid-range of 7.5%. WEC’s forward PEG ratio stands at 2.63X. To calculate the target price-to-earnings ratio, I will multiply the forward PEG ratio of 2.63 by the expected EPS growth rate (7.5%), giving us 19.73. WEC’s future price will be calculated by multiplying the projected EPS ($8) by the targeted P/E (19.73X), which equals $157.84. With the current share price at $111.32, it means that WEC is undervalued by about 41.8% (over the next 5 years).

I will compare the performance of WEC with some of its peers while targeting a 5-year EPS growth rate.

Forward. PEG ratio 5-year EPS growth rate Target P/E ratio Forward EPS Future Price Current Price Valuation (difference over 5 years) Dividend Yield
WEC 2.63 7.5% 19.73X $8 $157.84 $111.32 41.8% (undervalued) 3.42%
Alliant Energy Corporation. (LNT) 2.71 7.69% 20.84X $4.65 $96.91 $68.23 42.03% (undervalued) 3.15%
CMS Energy Corporation. (CMS) 2.53 7.54% 19.08X $5.54 $105.70 $75.42 40.15% (undervalued) 3.09%
Ameren Corporation. (AEE) 2.62 8.03% 21.04X $7.39 $155.49 $106.02 46.66% (undervalued) 2.84%

I have analyzed these stocks with the EPS forward growth rate of 5 years (CAGR). All stocks are undervalued by more than 40% in the projected period, but AEE has a higher possible upside of 46.66%.

I will further discount WEC's projected 2030 share price to its present value (PV) using the cost of equity (COE) criteria to determine its current worth relative to its future expected value.

Firstly, my tabulated analysis indicates that WEC's future price is $157.84. WEC's cost of equity is 7.29% (0.0729), based on a risk-free rate of 4.24%. The time or discounting period is 5 years. To get the present value, I will divide the future price ($157.84) by 1.0729 raised to 5 (derived from adding 1 to 0.0729), giving us $111.15 as the company's intrinsic value (a difference of 42%).

WEC is expected to post the highest EPS of $8, as compared to the other companies, against a PE ratio of almost 20X. It is followed by AEE at $7.39 with a possible share upside of 46.66%, then CMS and LNT.

Risk

WEC’s earnings growth projection is tied to the successful execution of its $37.5 billion capital plan. A delay or failure to inject the required capital will harm the valuation and growth of the share price.

Bottom Line

I have rated WEC Energy Group as a hold with a view to monitor its successful execution of its $37.5 billion capital plan. The valuation is based on its 5-year cumulative price appreciation and not a current discount. However, the successful implementation of this plan will grow the stock's momentum since the company expects to complete part of the Vantage project by 2028. In the next 5 years, I have noted that WEC’s EPS may reach a high of $8 and a possible share upside of 41.8%. Failure to execute this plan may harm the stock’s performance. That said, I await the Q1 2026 earnings release to update my analysis.

data.title: London Stock Exchange Group: Battered By Overblown AI Concerns
data.description:
AI-related concerns have weighed heavily on London Stock Exchange Group (LSEGY)(LDNXF)('LSEG') of late. Investors are worried that the company's data-heavy business model is ripe for disruption from startups like Anthropic, whose recent announcement of a new AI-enabled tool for automating legal work sent software and data-related stocks tumbling.

That has left LSEG's ADSs around 25% lower than they were at the beginning of last year – a number that actually includes some benefit from a weaker dollar. The stock has performed even worse in its native British pound, falling around 33% in that time.

And yet, LSEG's shares were sitting at a record high at the start of 2025. Have the fundamentals of the business really been upended in such a short space of time? Consensus estimates would suggest not, with analysts forecasting double-digit earnings growth when the company reports its 2025 results next week.

This sell-off has left LSEG's valuation looking depressed compared to peers and its own historical range. For investors prepared to block out the noise, this could be a good time to pick up a solid business at an attractive price. I rate shares "Strong Buy".

Overblown Data Fears

LSEG's name is a nod to its traditional business of managing one of the world's oldest stock exchanges. Equities, though, kicked in less than 5% of firm-wide income through the first nine months of 2025, while the entire Markets segment, which includes fixed income, FX, derivatives, and more, contributed less than 40% of total revenue.

LSEG's largest division is Data & Analytics, contributing roughly 45% to company-wide revenue. This is also where AI fears seem to be centered. The broad worry is that AI-enabled technology will eventually commoditize financial data, robbing data firms like LSEG of their pricing power.

In countering that view, it is important to appreciate that Data & Analytics division is itself very broad. Data & Feeds is around 45% of this revenue (so around 20% of group revenue). At one end of this sub-unit sits the truly non-proprietary offerings. This would include company fundamentals, filings, transcripts, and so on – data that is probably most vulnerable to AI. LSEG rarely sells this data on a standalone basis to begin with, so this already offers some protection from cheaper alternatives.

At the other end sits the more "moaty" data. Examples of this would be real time pricing and LSEG's vast trove of tick history. A researcher may, for instance, use the latter to conduct backtesting, while a trader could use it to develop an algorithmic strategy. Of course, LSEG also owns or partly owns exchanges, like its namesake and Tradeweb (TW), which can then strengthen this data offering. Importantly, only around 10% of Data & Feeds revenue comes from non-proprietary public data.

Similar logic applies to Workflows, which accounts for just under 50% of Data & Analytics' income (around 22% of group revenue). The main product of this unit is Workspace, which includes the physical terminals that compete with Bloomberg. Workspace customers will include wealth managers, investment bankers, traders, and so on.

A cheaper AI-driven product may appeal to some of these clients, but certainly not all. Trading, for example, accounts for much of this unit's revenue. Traders can have quite complex needs, and these needs often overlap with LSEG's other offerings (access to real-time price data, for example). With over 300,000 users, network effects are also strong here (traders can, for example, communicate via the messenger app to coordinate on a deal). To hammer that point home, all of the world's largest 100 banks are LSEG customers.

So, out of 45% of company revenue, we are really talking about a small minority that might be vulnerable to AI. The sell-side analysts that cover LSEG certainly don't appear to be worried. They see Data & Analytics revenue totaling £3.98 billion in 2025, growing to £4.40 billion by 2027.

The Wider Picture

With the market seemingly focused on the data side of things, it would be easy to lose sight of LSEG's other operations. One interesting part of its business is FTSE Russell, which contributes around 11% to total revenue. FTSE Russell is home to well-known indices like the Russell 1000. Income in this business comes from a mixture of subscriptions and asset-based revenue (from exchange-traded funds that track its indices, for example).

The rise of so-called "passive" investing is a nice structural tailwind. Managed assets in exchange-traded funds linked to a FTSE Russell index totaled $1.7 trillion at last count, up over 20% year-over-year. As a result, growth prospects for this business appear quite bright. FTSE Russell did £950 million in revenue over the four quarters through Q3 2025, and analysts expect this to rise to £1.12 billion by 2027.

Cost leverage is another attractive feature of this business. If $10 billion flows into an ETF that tracks a FTSE Russell index, that would result in some revenue growth for LSEG. However, costs don't need to rise in tandem to service that revenue, resulting in margin expansion and earnings growing faster than sales.

This dynamic also applies to the group. LSEG is expected to post £4.52 billion in adjusted EBITDA when it reports 2025 results at the end of the month, for a 50.3% margin. Analysts forecast this to to expand to 51.7% by 2027, resulting in 8% annualized EBITDA growth. Far from being disrupted, LSEG's growth prospects actually look quite healthy.

Shares At A Discount

LSEG's shares trade for £75.84 (around $26 per ADS) as I type, equal to 16.5x consensus 2026 earnings per share. This valuation looks relatively attractive. Firstly, it's on the cheaper end compared to other large exchange and financial data stocks.

It is also cheap compared to LSEG's own recent historical trading range. Between 2022 and 2025, for example, the stock averaged around 27 times trailing earnings.

If LSEG earns £4.60 per share in 2026 (around $1.60 per ADS), which is the current consensus estimate, and its shares trade for 20 times those earnings, which would still be a wide discount to its recent historical average, then today's buyer would be looking at over 20% price appreciation over the next twelve months. For investors prepared to ride out short-term AI-related volatility, LSEG looks like a compelling value pick. I rate shares "Strong Buy".

data.title: Boston Pizza: A Defensive Play In A Cooling Canadian Consumer Market
data.description:
Another quarter in the books, and the market still treats Boston Pizza (BPZZF) as nothing more than a royalty machine.

Since the upgrade to 'Buy,' unitholders have picked up nearly 20% in total returns versus just 6% for the S&P, collecting distributions along the way.

To be very honest with you, all my Canadian royalty fund picks have been working, especially now that the market seems to be rotating out of tech and back into value, particularly steady cash cows with predictable cash flow.

Just look at Diversified (DIV:CA), for example. Since I pointed out that the Cheba Hut deal could drive decades of inflation-linked royalty growth, the fund is already up just over 25%.

But don't forget that restaurant royalties aren't the safest investment in the world.

For distribution to continue growing, they need either same-store sales to continue growing or franchisees to open more restaurants for the royalty pool.

Boston Pizza hasn't had any net new openings in at least three years.

I told you last February that they were relying too heavily on same-store sales growth to sustain this increase in distribution. And that's by far the biggest risk for this royalty fund.

Things started to improve when they launched a prix fixe meal.

Same-store sales finally got a boost, and they were able to capitalize on the 'Fast-Food Exodus' movement from routine occasions, something many other full-service restaurants in the U.S. had done.

But with the macroeconomic scenario showing signs of deterioration (especially in January), what will happen to Boston Pizza?

I mean, if same-store sales growth is so important, will they be able to maintain it?

That's exactly what I want to try to answer here.

What's on the Canadian Table?

In my last write-ups, I told you that despite all the problems in the Canadian economy, the consumer remained strong and spent a lot of money on discretionary purchases, especially services.

It turns out that since then, some things have changed.

The main one, in my opinion, was the surge in food inflation in December to 6.2% (the highest annual inflation since mid-2023), affecting some high-value-perceived items such as meat, coffee, and chocolates.

The rationale here is quite simple. With larger grocery bills for everyday items, shoppers would likely save money on discretionary occasions and, to some extent, stop frequenting restaurants (especially the more expensive ones).

You can see that Discretionary Services (the category in which restaurants are included) fell 0.9% from December to January in the RBC Consumer Tracker, corroborating the slowdown thesis.

One thing that really caught my attention was that the Dining category was much more resilient. To give you an idea, in January it only fell 0.3% month-over-month (Grocery fell ~1% in the same period).

In this scenario (especially post-Christmas, where grocery chains are already passing price increases on to customers), I really feel that Boston Pizza could outperform again.

Think about it. Fixed-price deals offer perceived value and, more importantly, certainty. When people know what they're going to pay, the gap between convenience and value shrinks.

That's why Canadians keep going back to chains that offer this, and Boston Pizza enters right into it with 'Pasta Tuesday' and the '$15 Lunch.'

You're right to say that increases in beef prices squeeze unit-level economies for franchisees. But as long as they don't close their doors, you would still receive royalties on gross sales, not on restaurant-level EBITDA.

They can soften the impact by leaning into cheaper pasta and protein options within those deals, which they're already doing.

At lunch, for example, you'll see 8-inch personal pizzas, pasta dishes, or sandwiches.

In this setup, I, personally, see Boston Pizza hitting the same explosive store volumes, but in the low single digits, stealing market share from more expensive competitors.

Keeping an Eye on Distributions

The fund's revenue increased 4.2% to $9.8 million in the quarter due to franchise sales being at least $10.2 million higher in Q4 compared to the previous year.

And, as I said before, the royalty pool once again remained stagnant at 372 restaurants.

So, once again, same-store sales saved the day. This time, they came in at 3.7%—the weakest reading since the launch of the fixed-price promotion and a sequential slowdown of 40 bp.

Nothing to lose sleep over. My base case calls for low single-digit dividend growth, and as long as distributable cash supports that pace, it’s perfectly fine.

Here’s a chart for you to compare it to other Canadian chains:

They didn't break down this number, but we likely saw a small traffic gain with a low single-digit increase in the average check, primarily coming from price increases.

There were non-recurring drivers like the MLB Playoffs, but I think this is already part of the sports-centric business model they operate under.

At the bottom line, they generated a 5.1% increase in distributable cash during the quarter ($0.369 per unit). And this is what you like to see from a fund of this type, outperforming both same-store sales and royalty revenue in the quarter, indicating that they are seeing small cost efficiencies in the fund's structure.

Looking at the payout, you might even be a little scared. It stalled at 127.3% in Q4 and 101.9% for the entire FY 2025.

But if we remove the effects of the special dividend ($0.11 paid on December 18), the payout ratio stands at 97.5% in Q4 and 94.5% in FY 2025.

The current distribution is supported by the growth of distributable cash, but as I have been warning, only if same-store sales continue to grow in the low- to mid-single digits. Otherwise, they may slow to the low single digits.

Opportunities Ahead

Given the headwinds in Q1 FY 2026 that could push same-store sales into the low single digits, management told us they are preparing some actions to maintain momentum for the remainder of the year.

We have an array of exciting initiatives lined up for the first quarter of 2026 designed to maintain our strong sales momentum. I'll continue -- I'll share the details with you shortly.

Nothing was said, but we can speculate on a few things.

The first is the pipeline of new store openings—which, incidentally, hasn't moved for at least two years. At least two delayed openings from FY 2025 are now scheduled for this year, one in BC and one in Ontario.

Just as BJ's is seeing opportunities in markets with smaller DMAs like Brookfield in Wisconsin, Boston Pizza is targeting areas like Hope, British Columbia.

In that market, for example, you have less saturation, weaker competition (almost all are local chains), and community presence on game days.

I also see a slight tailwind in Q1 with the redemption of gift cards purchased in December with a $10 bonus, but it will hardly fully offset the effects of the storms.

Still a 'Buy'?

As I often say, the simple way to judge whether a royalty fund is cheap is to value the stream of cash it sends to unitholders in perpetuity and bring it back to today’s dollars. In plain terms, that's the Gordon Model.

With same-store sales running in the low single digits and distributable cash growing at a similar pace, it’s reasonable to expect distributions to follow that same path.

My base case here is distributions slowing down in perpetuity and converging to something close to ~2%.

Of course, we can model a more conservative scenario of ~1.5% to have some margin of safety here.

For the discount rate, I'm working with a cost of equity in the 6.8% to 7% range, using an 8% long-term TSX return, a 0.75 beta, and a 3.25% yield on the 10-year Canadian bond.

Plugging these estimates into my model, if dividends in FY 2026 are $1.55 per unit, my base scenario would give us a price target of ~$31 per unit.

If growth is 1.5%, the unit would be worth approximately $28 to $29.

At $24.30, the market would be pricing in a perpetual growth rate of only 0.5% from a discount rate of 6.8%.

I will reiterate my 'Buy' rating on this royalty fund targeting +25% upside.

I do expect same-store sales to cool off, and distributions will probably follow at some point. But I don't see a sharp drop, especially with payout coverage, excluding special dividends, still looking healthy.
